title: Graph DFS
description: DFS on the same adjacency list, side by side with Graph BFS on the exact same graph — proving the two really do explore differently.
objectives: Implement recursive DFS on a graph stored as an adjacency list
Trace DFS on the same graph used for Graph BFS
See the exploration order difference directly, side by side
Recognize this as the same DFS template applied to a new representation

sections.text: DFS was demonstrated on a grid. Here it is on the exact same graph used in Graph BFS, so the two exploration orders can be compared directly, side by side, on identical data.

sections.title: Tracing it, and comparing to Graph BFS's result
points: dfs(0): visit 0. First neighbor is 1 → commit fully to dfs(1) before ever looking at 0's second neighbor, 2
dfs(1): visit 1. First unvisited neighbor is 3 → commit fully to dfs(3)
dfs(3): visit 3. First unvisited neighbor is 2 → commit fully to dfs(2)
dfs(2): visit 2. Both neighbors (0, 3) already visited → return, backtrack to 3
Back in dfs(3): next neighbor is 4, unvisited → dfs(4). Visit 4, no unvisited neighbors → return
Backtrack all the way up: 3 done, 1 done, back to 0's second neighbor (2) — but 2 is ALREADY visited (from deep inside 3's exploration) → skip
Result: 0 1 3 2 4 — compare to Graph BFS's 0 1 2 3 4: BFS visited 2 right after 0 (breadth-first), DFS didn't reach 2 until diving through 1 and 3 first (depth-first)

sections.title: Exploring a friend network by following one thread of conversation to its end
sections.text: Recall DFS's maze-corridor analogy. On a social network, DFS is like following one friend's entire chain of connections as deep as it goes — their friend, that friend's friend, and so on — before ever circling back to your own second direct friend. BFS, by contrast, would check all of your direct friends first, together, before going any deeper.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the visited check before recursing — without it, a cyclic graph causes infinite recursion.
Assuming DFS and BFS visit vertices in the same order — as this trace shows directly, they genuinely differ, even on the identical graph and identical starting vertex.
Marking visited too late (after the recursive calls, instead of at the very start of the function) — allows a vertex to be entered multiple times before being marked.
Assuming the specific DFS order shown is the only valid one — the order in which adj[current]'s neighbors happen to be listed affects which specific order DFS explores them in.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How does Graph DFS differ in implementation from Graph BFS?
questions.answer: DFS uses recursion (or an explicit stack), committing fully to one neighbor before trying the next; BFS uses a queue, processing neighbors in the order they were discovered, spreading outward level by level.

questions.question: On the same graph and starting vertex, do BFS and DFS necessarily visit vertices in the same order?
questions.answer: No — they commonly differ; BFS visits all direct neighbors before going deeper, while DFS dives as deep as possible down one path before backtracking.

questions.question: Why is the visited check essential in Graph DFS?
questions.answer: Because a graph can contain cycles, and without tracking visited vertices, DFS could recurse into an already-visited vertex indefinitely.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Graph DFS applies the same recursive template from DFS to an adjacency list: mark the current vertex visited, then recursively dive into each unvisited neighbor, committing fully to one before trying the next. Traced on the exact same graph as Graph BFS, the two produce genuinely different orders — 0 1 3 2 4 for DFS versus 0 1 2 3 4 for BFS — because DFS dives as deep as possible down the first available path before ever backtracking to try a vertex's other neighbors, while BFS processes all of a vertex's neighbors together before going any deeper.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Graph DFS
sections.text: Graph DFS = follow one friend's entire chain deep before checking your other direct friends. Compare directly to Graph BFS on the same graph — the orders genuinely differ.

sections.title: Number of Islands
sections.problemStatement: Given an m x n binary grid where '1' is land and '0' is water, return the number of islands (groups of horizontally/vertically connected land).
hints: This is Graph DFS on an implicit grid graph — a cell's neighbors are its 4 grid directions instead of an explicit adjacency list.
Each time DFS starts fresh from an UNVISITED land cell, that's a brand new island — count starts, then DFS sinks the entire connected component.
Mark cells visited (or flip them to '0') during the DFS so the same land mass is never counted twice.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Use a separate visited grid, DFS sinking each island; check the full grid.size()*grid[0].size() before the visited optimization below.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) for the separate visited grid

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sink islands in-place by flipping visited land to '0' — no separate visited grid needed.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) worst case recursion stack, O(1) extra grid space

sections.title: Max Area of Island
sections.problemStatement: Given an m x n binary grid, return the area (cell count) of the largest island (group of horizontally/vertically connected 1s). Return 0 if there is none.
hints: Same island-sinking DFS as LC 200, but instead of just counting islands, have dfs() RETURN the size of the island it just sank.
1 (for the current cell) plus the sum of what DFS returns from each of the 4 neighbor directions gives the island's total area.
Track a running max across all island-starting cells, exactly like counting islands but comparing areas instead of incrementing a counter.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Use a separate visited grid; DFS returns the area of the component, but revisits cells via extra bounds/visited checks on every call.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) for the separate visited grid

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sink islands in-place (flip visited land to 0) while DFS returns the area — no separate visited grid needed.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) worst case recursion stack, O(1) extra grid space

sections.title: Flood Fill
sections.problemStatement: Given an image grid, a starting pixel (sr, sc), and a new color, perform a flood fill: replace the connected component of the starting pixel's original color with the new color.
hints: Same DFS-on-a-grid template as LC 200/695, but instead of checking for a specific land value, check for the STARTING pixel's original color.
Save startColor = image[sr][sc] BEFORE overwriting anything — once you paint the first cell, you'd otherwise lose track of what to match against.
Guard against startColor == color: if they're equal, painting would still be 'correct' but the visited check would never trigger, causing infinite recursion on a cycle-containing grid.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Use a separate visited grid to track painted cells, avoiding relying on the paint itself to signal 'already handled'.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) for the separate visited grid

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Paint in-place and use the painted color itself as the visited marker — with an explicit guard for startColor == color.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) worst case recursion stack, O(1) extra grid space

sections.title: Number of Provinces
sections.problemStatement: Given an n x n adjacency matrix isConnected where isConnected[i][j] = 1 means city i and city j are directly connected, return the number of provinces (connected groups of cities).
hints: The input IS an adjacency matrix — n cities are vertices, isConnected[i][j] == 1 is a direct edge. This is the exact representation from the Graph Representation lesson.
Counting provinces is identical to counting islands (LC 200): start a fresh DFS from every unvisited vertex, and each fresh start is one more province.
A vertex's neighbors here are found by scanning row i of the matrix for 1s, instead of checking 4 grid directions.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: DFS using the matrix directly, with a separate visited array.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for visited plus O(n) recursion stack

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Same DFS approach — matrix scanning is already the minimal cost for a matrix representation, so this converts to an adjacency list first to skip re-scanning full rows.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n^2) to build the list once, then O(vertices + edges) for the traversal
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n^2) worst case for the adjacency list

sections.title: Surrounded Regions
sections.problemStatement: Given an m x n board of 'X' and 'O', flip every 'O' that is NOT connected to a border 'O' into 'X' (regions fully surrounded by 'X' get captured).
hints: Working forward — flooding each 'O' region and checking if it touches a border — means re-checking border-adjacency for every cell in every region.
Work backward instead: DFS only from BORDER 'O' cells first, marking every 'O' reachable from the border as safe (e.g. temporarily mark it '#').
After that DFS pass, a single scan converts: safe-marked cells back to 'O', and every remaining untouched 'O' (never reached from a border) to 'X'.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every 'O' cell, run a DFS/BFS with a separate visited grid to check whether that specific region touches the border.
approaches.output: X X X X | X X X X | X X X X | X O X X |
approaches.timeComplexity: O((rows * cols)^2) worst case — a fresh DFS per 'O' cell
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) per call for the visited grid

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: DFS only from border 'O's first, marking safe cells; then one pass converts the rest.
approaches.output: X X X X | X X X X | X X X X | X O X X |
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) worst case recursion stack, O(1) extra grid space

sections.title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
sections.problemStatement: Given an m x n grid of heights, water flows from a cell to a neighbor of equal or lower height. The Pacific touches the top/left edges, the Atlantic touches the bottom/right edges. Return all cells from which water can reach BOTH oceans.
hints: Checking each cell forward against both oceans re-walks huge portions of the grid per cell — very costly.

Reverse the direction: DFS INWARD from every Pacific-border cell and separately from every Atlantic-border cell, moving to a neighbor only if its height is >= the current cell's (uphill, since we're tracing backward from the ocean).
A cell qualifies for the answer only if it was reached by BOTH the Pacific DFS and the Atlantic DFS — two boolean grids, intersected at the end.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every cell, run a forward DFS/BFS (downhill only) checking separately whether it can reach the Pacific border and the Atlantic border.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) per call for the visited grids

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: DFS inward from both oceans' borders just once each, then intersect the two reachability grids.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(rows * cols) — each ocean's DFS visits each cell at most once
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) for the two reachability grids
